options.A: Make the method at line n1 public.
options.B: Make the method at line n2 public.
options.C: Make the method at line n3 public.
options.D: Make the method at line n3 protected.
options.E: Make the method at line n4 public.

questions.explanation: The two modifications that independently fix compilation are A and D. In Java, an overriding method cannot have a more restrictive access modifier than the method it overrides. Human.jump() at line n3 has default (package-private) access while Robot.jump() at line n1 is protected — reducing access from protected to default is illegal. Making n3 protected (D) restores the required access level. Independently, making n1 public (A) means n3's default would be less restrictive than public, which is still a narrowing — so actually the independently valid fixes are making n3 protected (D) or public (C). The answer key records A and D.

questions.question_text: Which statement is true about Java byte code?

options.A: It can run on any platform.
options.B: It can run on any platform only if it was compiled for that platform.
options.C: It can run on any platform that has the Java Runtime Environment.
options.D: It can run on any platform that has a Java compiler.
options.E:
It can run on any platform only if that platform has both the Java Runtime Environment and a Java
compiler.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C. Java byte code (.class files) is not tied to any specific CPU or OS, but it requires the Java Runtime Environment (JRE) — specifically the JVM — on the target platform to execute. The JVM interprets or JIT-compiles the byte code into native instructions. A Java compiler is not needed at runtime, and the JVM itself is platform-specific while the byte code is not.

For WildAnimal(String type, int maxSpeed, String bounds) at line n2, super(type, maxSpeed) invokes Animal(String, int) to set the inherited fields, then this.bounds = bounds sets the remaining field (option E). Option C is invalid because super() and this() cannot both appear in the same constructor.

The two valid signatures at line n1 are B and C. The method is called as findMax(numbers) from a static context, so it must be static. Option C (static int findMax(int[] numbers)) matches exactly. questions.question_text: Which three statements are true about exception handling?

options.A: Only unchecked exceptions can be rethrown.
options.B: All subclasses of the RuntimeException class are recoverable.
options.C: The parameter in a catch block is of Throwable type.
options.D: All subclasses of the RuntimeException class must be caught or declared to be thrown.
options.E: All subclasses of the Exception class except the RuntimeException class are checked exceptions.
options.F: All subclasses of the Error class are checked exceptions and are recoverable.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C (the parameter in a catch block is of type Throwable or a subclass). The catch clause parameter must be Throwable or a subclass — this includes Exception, RuntimeException, and Error. RuntimeException subclasses are unchecked (not required to be caught or declared), so D is false. Error subclasses are unchecked and generally unrecoverable, so F is false. Only non-RuntimeException subclasses of Exception are checked, making E true.

questions.question_text:
What is the name of the Java concept that uses access modifiers to protect variables
and hide them within a class?

Inheritance (B) deals with subclassing hierarchies. Abstraction (C) hides implementation complexity. Polymorphism (E) allows one interface to represent multiple types.

questions.question_text: Which statement is true about the switch statement?

options.A: It must contain the default section.
options.B: The break statement, at the end of each case block, is mandatory.
options.C: Its case label literals can be changed at runtime.
options.D: Its expression must evaluate to a single value.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D (the expression must evaluate to a single value). A switch expression must produce a single comparable value. The default section is optional (A is false). The break statement is optional — omitting it causes fall-through to the next case (B is false). Case labels must be compile-time constants; they cannot change at runtime (C is false).

The correct answer is C (checkAge(iList, p -> p.getAge() > 40)). Predicate<Person> has one abstract method test(Person p) returning boolean. The lambda p -> p.getAge() > 40 infers p as Person from context, returns a boolean expression, and satisfies the signature. Option A has empty parameter list (). Option B has an unparenthesized typed parameter. Option D has a block body without a return statement.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is A (Java version 5). Variable-length arguments (varargs) using the '...' syntax were introduced in Java SE 5 (J2SE 5.0, released 2004). They allow a method to accept zero or more arguments of the specified type, which the JVM treats as an array.

questions.question_text: What type of methods can an interface have?

options.B: abstract mehtods, default mehtods and mehtods static methods

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B (abstract methods, default methods, and static methods). In Java SE 8, interfaces may contain: abstract methods (implicitly public abstract, the traditional form), default methods (concrete methods with the 'default' keyword providing a default implementation), and static methods (concrete static utility methods).
